name: Bougainvillea
description: Tropical flowering vine/shrub with vibrant papery bracts in red, pink, orange, yellow, or purple. Highly drought-tolerant once established and blooms prolifically in warm climates.
daysToBloom: 30-90 days; blooms year-round in frost-free zones
careNotes: Minimal water after establishment. Avoid overwatering. Prune for shape and to encourage flowering. Very heat and salt tolerant.

name: Hibiscus (Tropical)
description: Tropical shrub producing large, showy flowers in red, pink, orange, yellow, coral, or bi-colors. Blooms nearly year-round in warm zones with prominent stamens.
careNotes: Needs well-draining soil. Susceptible to pests in humidity. Regular feeding promotes blooms. Prune to maintain shape.

name: Pentas
description: Heat and humidity-loving flowering shrub with star-shaped flowers in red, pink, white, or purple. Blooms prolifically and attracts pollinators like butterflies.
careNotes: Shear back by one-third when leggy. Deadheading extends bloom. Minimal fertilizer needed.

name: Ixora
description: Tropical shrub with dense clusters of small flowers in orange, red, pink, or yellow. Attracts butterflies and produces persistent glossy foliage.
careNotes: Excellent drainage essential. Root rot from overwatering is the #1 problem. Plant in well-draining sandy soil.

name: Lantana
description: Heat-loving tropical shrub with clusters of small flowers that change color as they mature. Highly attractive to butterflies and hummingbirds.
careNotes: Deadheading promotes continuous blooms. Tolerates poor soil. Sterile cultivars recommended to prevent invasiveness.

name: Firebush
description: Native Florida shrub with tubular red to orange flowers and dark foliage. Attracts hummingbirds and butterflies prolifically.
careNotes: Self-seeds readily. Tolerates poor, sandy soil. Minimal fertilizer. Florida native plant.

name: Blanket Flower
description: Native Florida wildflower with bi-colored red and yellow daisy-like flowers. Long blooming season and extremely low-maintenance once established.
careNotes: Requires excellent drainage and sandy soil. No fertilizer needed. Deadheading extends bloom.

name: Scarlet Sage
description: Native perennial with striking scarlet-red tubular flowers on tall spikes. Attracts hummingbirds and butterflies. Florida native.
careNotes: Self-seeds in gardens. Minimal fertilizer. Deadheading promotes continued blooming. Very heat tolerant.

name: Coreopsis
description: Cheerful wildflower with bi-colored red and gold daisy-like flowers. Low-maintenance perennial with fine, wispy foliage.
careNotes: Requires excellent drainage and sandy soil. No fertilizer needed. Self-seeds readily.

name: Lily of the Nile
description: Tropical perennial producing spherical clusters of small blue or purple lily-like flowers on tall stems. Striking architectural plant.
careNotes: Prefers well-drained soil. Forms clumps over time. Drought-tolerant once established. Salt-tolerant for coastal gardens.

name: Nasturtium
description: Annual with peppery-tasting edible flowers and leaves. Brilliant yellow, orange, or red flowers with shield-shaped foliage. Both flowers and leaves are edible.
careNotes: Do not fertilize (excess suppresses flowering). Well-drained soil essential. Also useful as companion plant for pest control.

name: Borage
description: Annual herb with distinctive blue star-shaped edible flowers and hairy stems. Flowers taste like cucumber and attract pollinators.
careNotes: Well-drained soil. Self-seeds readily. Harvest flowers in the morning for best flavor.

name: Squash Blossoms
description: Large, delicate orange or yellow flowers from summer squash plants. Male flowers can be harvested for culinary use with a mild vegetable flavor.
careNotes: Harvest male flowers in early morning. Pick before female flowers for continuous harvest. Needs well-drained soil.

name: Roselle (Edible Hibiscus)
description: Edible hibiscus producing flowers and calyces with a tart, cranberry-like flavor. Commonly used to make herbal tea (agua de jamaica) and jams.
careNotes: Same care as ornamental hibiscus. Calyces can be dried for storage and tea. Drought-tolerant once established.

name: Mexican Mint Marigold
description: Aromatic herb with small yellow/orange edible flowers and tarragon-like foliage with sweet licorice notes. Excellent tarragon substitute for Florida gardens.
careNotes: Perennial in frost-free zones. Attracts pollinators. Used for tea and culinary purposes. Can be invasive if not managed.

name: Okra
description: Tropical plant with beautiful yellow hibiscus-like edible flowers. Both flowers and immature pods are edible. Thrives in Florida heat.
careNotes: Very heat-tolerant. Harvest pods at 2-3 inches daily. Flowers are edible and ornamental.

name: Tomato
description: Warm-season fruiting plant producing round edible fruits. In Zone 10b, tomatoes are grown in the cool season (fall through spring) as summer heat stops production.
careNotes: Avoid planting after April. Summer heat stops fruit set. Needs well-draining soil with organic matter. Stake or cage for support. Mulch to retain moisture.

name: Pepper
description: Warm-season plant producing bell or hot peppers. Grows slowly but productively in warm, full-sun locations. Loves the Florida heat.
careNotes: Loves heat. Needs well-draining soil. Stake taller varieties. Mulch heavily. Watch for spider mites in dry conditions.

name: Cucumber
description: Warm-season trailing vine producing crisp, green fruits. In Zone 10b, plant in fall through early spring to avoid summer heat stress.
careNotes: Provide trellis for vertical growth. Harvest regularly for continuous production. Too hot May-August. Keep soil consistently moist.

name: Eggplant
description: Warm-season plant producing purple, green, or white oblong edible fruits. Loves heat even more than tomatoes and is very productive in Florida.
careNotes: Loves heat more than tomatoes. Rich, well-draining soil. Mulch heavily. Stake tall varieties. Succession plant for continuous harvest.

name: Lettuce
description: Cool-season leafy green producing tender edible leaves. Must be grown in winter in Zone 10b as it bolts quickly in heat above 75°F.
careNotes: Ideal temp 45-70°F. Use shade cloth in late February. Succession plant every 2-3 weeks. Prefers rich soil.

name: Kale
description: Cold-hardy leafy green with crinkled or curled leaves. Flavor actually improves after cool weather. Very nutritious and long-producing.
careNotes: Very hardy. Slow to bolt. Harvest outer leaves continuously. Prefers nitrogen-rich soil. Few pests or diseases in Florida.

name: Sweet Potato
description: Warm-season vine crop producing nutritious sweet tubers. Tolerant of poor soil and heat. One of the best warm-season crops for Florida.
careNotes: Plant slips (sprouts), not whole tubers. Loose, well-draining soil. Hill soil around vines. Excess fertilizer promotes vines over tubers.

name: Squash / Zucchini
description: Warm-season vine producing large edible fruits. Very productive; plants produce heavily once established. Avoid peak summer heat.
careNotes: Provide trellis. Harvest young for best quality. Succession plant every 2-3 weeks. Needs rich soil with organic matter.

name: Swiss Chard
description: Nearly year-round producer with colorful stalks (white, yellow, orange, pink, red) and dark green leaves. One of Zone 10b's most reliable crops.
careNotes: Closest to a true year-round crop in Zone 10b. Tolerates both heat and mild cold. Slow to bolt. Harvest outer leaves for regrowth.

name: Spinach
description: Cool-season leafy green with tender dark green leaves. Extremely heat-sensitive; must be planted in the coolest months in Zone 10b.
careNotes: Very cold-hardy but bolts fast in heat. Ideal 50-70°F. Stop planting after January 15. Succession plant every 2-3 weeks.

name: Carrot
description: Cool-season root crop producing sweet orange edible roots. Requires loose, well-draining soil for proper root development.
careNotes: Needs loose soil (heavy soil produces forked roots). Thin seedlings. Consistent moisture prevents bitter roots. Succession plant every 3-4 weeks.

name: Mango
description: Large tropical fruit tree producing sweet aromatic stone fruits. One of the world's most important tropical fruits. Grafted trees fruit in about 4 years.
daysToBloom: Blooms Dec-Mar; fruit matures 3-5 months later (Jun-Aug)
careNotes: Grafted trees fruit in 4 years (seed trees: 5-8 years). Grows well in sandy Florida soil. Select cold-hardy varieties like 'Tommy Atkins'. Avoid planting late May-early July.

name: Avocado
description: Tropical fruit tree producing creamy, nutrient-rich pear-shaped fruits. Must have excellent drainage; dies in waterlogged soil.
careNotes: Must have excellent drainage (dies in wet soil). 'Bacon' and 'Fuerte' recommended for Zone 10b. Mulch around base. Wind protection helpful.

name: Papaya
description: Fast-growing tropical producing large melon-like yellow fruits rich in vitamins and digestive enzymes. Produces fruit within 9-12 months.
careNotes: Dies at 32°F; needs frost protection. Fast-growing (8-10 ft). Short-lived (5-7 years). Needs wind protection. Self-seeds readily.

name: Banana
description: Large herbaceous plant producing bunches of sweet yellow fruits. Multiple plantings provide year-round harvest. Not actually a tree.
careNotes: 'Cavendish' and 'Grand Nain' recommended. Dies back in freeze but regrows from rhizome. Needs wind protection. Heavy feeder. Remove old plant after harvest.

name: Citrus (Lime, Lemon, Orange)
description: Evergreen fruit trees producing juicy citrus fruits. Multiple blooming cycles in Zone 10b extend harvest seasons. A Florida classic.
daysToBloom: Grafted trees fruit within 3-4 years; fruit ripens 4-6 months after bloom
careNotes: Well-drained soil essential. Select cold-hardy cultivars. Multiple varieties provide nearly continuous harvest. Watch for Asian citrus psyllid pest.

name: Pineapple
description: Tropical herbaceous plant producing large sweet aromatic fruits. Unique spiky rosette form is ornamental and productive. Nearly care-free.
careNotes: Propagate from offsets or crowns. Excellent drainage (sandy soil ideal). Very salt-tolerant and pest-resistant. 'Golden Glow' thrives in Zone 10b.

name: Guava
description: Tropical tree producing small round fruits with white, pink, or red flesh. Extremely hardy, productive, and low-maintenance. Outstanding vitamin C source.
careNotes: Extremely hardy and low-maintenance. Grows in poor soil. Tolerates drought, pests, and diseases. Can self-seed and become weedy.

name: Passion Fruit
description: Fast-growing tropical vine producing distinctive purple-skinned fruits with aromatic seeds and juice. Extremely ornamental flowers.
careNotes: Vine needs strong trellis/support. Fast-growing and vigorous. Fruit ripens when wrinkled and drops. Very ornamental flowers. Few pests in Florida.

name: Lychee
description: Tropical tree producing marble-sized fruits with bumpy red skin and sweet translucent white flesh. Exotic flavor and attractive tree.
daysToBloom: Grafted: 4-5 years; blooms Dec-Feb, fruit Jun-Jul
careNotes: 'Brewster' and 'Mauritius' recommended. Grafted trees fruit much faster. Mulch heavily. Benefits from slight drought stress to trigger flowering.

name: Dragon Fruit (Pitaya)
description: Exotic tropical cactus producing large fruits with bright pink skin and white or magenta flesh. Night-blooming flowers are spectacular.
daysToBloom: Blooms May-Oct; fruit ripens 30-50 days after bloom
careNotes: Needs excellent drainage (sandy soil/cactus mix). Avoid overwatering. Needs strong support structure. Night-blooming flowers. Very pest-resistant.

name: Plumeria (Frangipani)
description: Iconic tropical tree producing intensely fragrant flowers in white, yellow, pink, and red. A quintessential Florida landscape tree used in leis and tropical arrangements. Blooms prolifically in warm months and goes dormant in winter.
careNotes: Excellent drainage essential — root rot is deadly. Stop watering in winter during dormancy. Propagate easily from dried cuttings. Very salt-tolerant. Watch for plumeria rust (orange powder on leaves).

name: Bird of Paradise
description: Dramatic tropical perennial with distinctive orange and blue flowers resembling an exotic bird in flight. Bold architectural foliage year-round. Extremely long-lived and low-maintenance once established.
daysToBloom: 3-5 years from division; blooms fall through spring
careNotes: Very drought-tolerant once established. Dislikes root disturbance — avoid transplanting. Divide clumps every 5-6 years to rejuvenate. Remove spent flower stalks. Minimal fertilizer needed.

name: Plumbago
description: A Florida favorite — fast-growing shrub covered in sky-blue or white flowers nearly year-round. Drought-tolerant once established and a magnet for butterflies. One of the most reliable bloomers in Zone 10b.
careNotes: Hard prune 2-3 times per year to keep compact and promote blooming. Very heat and drought tolerant. Can spread aggressively — give it space. Sticky seed pods cling to clothing and pet fur.

name: Portulaca (Moss Rose)
description: Low-growing annual succulent bursting with jewel-toned flowers in red, orange, yellow, pink, and white. Thrives in Florida's hottest, driest conditions where other flowers fail. Flowers open fully in sunshine and close at night.
careNotes: Loves heat, sandy soil, and neglect. Overwatering is the only way to kill it. Self-seeds prolifically. Excellent for rock gardens, borders, and containers. No deadheading needed — self-cleans.

name: Tropical Milkweed
description: Vibrant red-and-yellow flowering perennial and the primary host plant for Monarch and Queen butterflies in Florida. Blooms nearly year-round in Zone 10b. Easy to grow from seed and important for butterfly conservation.
careNotes: Cut back hard in November to prevent OE parasite buildup on Monarchs. Deer-resistant and spreads by seed. Grows in poor soil. Native milkweed species are preferred for conservation.

name: Blue Daze
description: Florida-native low-growing perennial smothered in powder-blue morning-glory-like flowers from spring through fall. Excellent groundcover, border, and container plant. Heat, drought, and salt tolerant.
careNotes: Trim back lightly to keep tidy. Excellent drainage required — does not tolerate wet feet. Very low-maintenance once established. Great for slopes, rock gardens, and hanging baskets.

name: Turk's Cap
description: Native Florida shrub producing distinctive twisted red flowers that never fully open — resembling a Turkish turban. Hummingbirds and butterflies love it. Thrives in extreme heat, drought, and even deep shade.
careNotes: One of the most adaptable plants for Florida. Grows in deep shade where others fail. Hard prune in late winter. Small red berries are edible with a mild hibiscus flavor. True Florida native.

name: Canna Lily
description: Bold tropical perennial with large paddle-shaped leaves (green, purple, or variegated) and dramatic flowers in red, orange, yellow, and pink. Grows rapidly in Florida's heat and can reach 3-6 feet tall.
careNotes: Divide rhizomes every 2-3 years. Deadhead spent blooms. Mulch to retain moisture. Watch for canna leafrollers. Remove old stalks after blooming. Loves moisture but tolerates brief dry spells.

name: Crinum Lily
description: Large striking perennial producing clusters of fragrant white or pink spider-like flowers on tall stalks with bold strap-like foliage year-round. Extremely tough and long-lived — clumps last for decades.
daysToBloom: Blooms spring through fall; mature bulbs bloom reliably
careNotes: Extremely durable — salt-tolerant and hurricane-resistant. Plant bulb with the top third above soil. Divide large clumps every 5 years. Deer and pest resistant. Remove spent flower stalks.

name: Gardenia
description: Beloved subtropical shrub prized for intensely fragrant pure-white flowers and rich, glossy evergreen foliage. One of the most intoxicatingly scented plants you can grow in a Zone 10b garden.
daysToBloom: Blooms mainly spring-summer; repeat bloom possible
careNotes: Needs acidic soil (pH 5.0-6.0) — use acidic fertilizer. Yellowing leaves indicate iron deficiency (add chelated iron). Susceptible to whiteflies and scale. Avoid planting near concrete (raises soil pH).

name: Heliconia
description: Dramatic tropical perennial with large banana-like leaves and spectacular lobster-claw or hanging flower bracts in red, orange, and yellow. A showstopper in any tropical garden. Loved by hummingbirds.
careNotes: Needs consistent moisture and rich, organic soil. Spreads via rhizomes — can become large. Remove spent stalks at the base. Protect from cold (frost-sensitive). Mulch heavily to retain moisture.

name: Ginger Lily (White Butterfly Ginger)
description: Lush tropical perennial producing intensely fragrant white butterfly-like flowers on tall reed-like canes. One of the most fragrant plants you can grow in Florida. State flower of Cuba. Naturalizes readily in moist areas.
daysToBloom: Blooms summer-fall; fragrant in the evening
careNotes: Loves moisture — great near ponds or in low wet areas. Cut old canes to the ground after flowering. Spreads via rhizomes. Very fast-growing in Florida's heat. May naturalize in moist areas.

name: Green Beans
description: Warm-season bush or pole beans producing tender edible pods. Very productive in Zone 10b during the cooler months. Bush types mature faster; pole types produce longer. Yard-long beans are especially suited to Florida heat.
careNotes: Direct sow only — taproot is transplant-sensitive. Provide trellis for pole varieties. Harvest frequently for continued production. Inoculate with rhizobium bacteria for best yields. Avoid overhead watering.

name: Collard Greens
description: Nutritious cool-season leafy green with large smooth dark green leaves. The most heat-tolerant of the brassicas — tolerates mild frosts and some late-spring warmth. A Southern staple that thrives in Florida winters.
careNotes: More heat-tolerant than kale or cabbage. Harvest outer leaves for continuous production. Nitrogen-rich soil produces the best leaves. Watch for cabbage loopers and aphids.

name: Arugula
description: Fast-growing cool-season green with peppery, nutty-flavored leaves. One of the quickest vegetables to harvest — ready in as little as 30 days. Must be planted in Zone 10b's cooler months as it bolts quickly in heat.
careNotes: Succession plant every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvest. Bolts and turns bitter above 65°F. Harvest outer leaves or whole plant. Shade cloth extends season into early March.

name: Radish
description: The fastest-maturing vegetable in the garden — some varieties ready in just 25 days! Cool-season root crop with crisp, peppery edible roots. Excellent for succession planting through Florida's entire winter season.
careNotes: Direct sow only. Thin to 2-3 inches apart for proper root formation. Harvest promptly when mature — they become pithy and overly hot if left. Loose, well-draining soil essential for round roots.

name: Broccoli
description: Cool-season vegetable producing dense, nutritious green flower heads in Florida's mild winters. 'Waltham 29' and 'Green Goliath' perform best in Zone 10b. After the main head is harvested, side shoots continue producing for weeks.
careNotes: Start transplants 6-8 weeks before the cool season. Consistent moisture and rich soil are key. Harvest main head before flowers open. Watch for cabbage loopers and aphids on leaves.

name: Green Onions / Scallions
description: Easy-to-grow bunching onion producing mild-flavored green tops and small white bulbs. One of the most versatile vegetables for Florida gardens — productive nearly year-round in Zone 10b with almost no care.
daysToBloom: 60-70 days from seed; 20-30 days from transplant
careNotes: Plant sets or transplants for fastest harvest. Harvest outer stalks while leaving the plant to regrow. Can regrow from grocery store bunches set in water. Well-draining fertile soil. Divide clumps regularly.

name: Pigeon Peas
description: Tropical perennial shrub producing edible peas — one of the best nitrogen-fixing crops for Florida. Extremely drought-tolerant and pest-resistant, growing to 6-10 feet. A staple food across the Caribbean and a soil-builder for the garden.
daysToBloom: 120-180 days to harvest; blooms fall, harvest winter
careNotes: Excellent nitrogen fixer that improves soil fertility. Very drought-tolerant once established. Harvest green for fresh use or let dry on plant for dried peas. Can double as a windbreak or privacy hedge.

name: Bitter Melon
description: Tropical vine producing distinctive warty green fruits used extensively in Asian, Caribbean, and Indian cuisines. Thrives in Florida's heat and humidity. Has significant medicinal properties — used in traditional medicine to support blood sugar levels.
careNotes: Provide a strong trellis — vigorous climber that can reach 15 feet. Harvest while still green (yellow means overripe and intensely bitter). Soak seeds 24 hours before planting. Very productive in summer heat.

name: Chayote
description: Tropical perennial vine in the squash family producing pear-shaped light green fruits. Extremely productive — one plant yields 50-100+ fruits per season. Leaves, shoots, and roots are also edible. A common vegetable throughout Latin America and the Caribbean.
daysToBloom: 120-150 days to first fruit; produces continuously thereafter
careNotes: Plant the whole fruit (including seed) directly in the ground. Needs very strong trellis — a vigorous grower. Perennial in Zone 10b. Cut back after fruiting season. Harvest fruits at 4-6 inches.

name: Cassava / Yuca
description: Tropical shrub grown for its large starchy edible roots. One of the most drought-tolerant food crops in the world and a staple across the Caribbean, Latin America, and Africa. Thrives in Florida's heat with minimal care or inputs.
careNotes: Plant stem cuttings horizontally 2-3 inches deep. Sandy, well-draining soil is ideal. Extremely drought-tolerant once established. Must be properly cooked before eating — raw contains cyanogenic compounds. Sweet varieties are safest.

name: Beets
description: Cool-season root vegetable producing sweet earthy roots and nutritious edible greens. Both the root and leafy tops are delicious. 'Detroit Dark Red' and 'Chioggia' varieties perform well in Florida's mild winters.
careNotes: Direct sow only — taproot is transplant-sensitive. Thin to 3-4 inches (thinnings are edible). Loose, rock-free soil for round roots. Consistent moisture prevents cracking. Harvest at 1.5-3 inches diameter.

name: Snow Peas / Sugar Snap Peas
description: Cool-season climbing vine producing sweet edible flat pods (snow peas) or plump sweet pods (sugar snap). Must be planted in Florida's coolest months. Brief but rewarding harvest window with very sweet flavor straight from the vine.
careNotes: Must finish before warm weather — stop planting after January. Provide trellis. Inoculate with rhizobium bacteria for better yields. Direct sow only. Harvest snow peas before seeds develop; snap peas when pods are plump.

name: Calabaza (Cuban Squash)
description: Large tropical pumpkin-like squash common throughout the Caribbean and Latin America. Produces 5-20 lb fruits with sweet orange flesh. More heat-tolerant than Northern varieties and can produce in Zone 10b's warm season.
careNotes: Vines spread widely — give plenty of space or grow vertically. Harvest when skin hardens and stem dries. Stores well for months at room temperature. Used in soups, stews, and desserts throughout the Caribbean.

name: Luffa (Loofah)
description: Tropical vine producing edible young fruits and natural sponges (loofah) when mature and dried. Florida's long warm season is perfect for this crop. Young fruits taste like zucchini; mature fruits dry into the fibrous sponges used in bathing.
careNotes: Needs very strong trellis — heavy vines and fruits. Start indoors 4 weeks early for longer growing season. Harvest young (6-8 inches) for eating. For sponges, leave on vine until skin turns brown and papery, then peel.

name: Starfruit (Carambola)
description: Tropical fruit tree producing uniquely star-shaped fruits with sweet-tart watery flesh. Beautiful ornamental tree with pink flowers and attractive foliage. Thrives in South Florida and produces 2-3 crops per year.
daysToBloom: Grafted trees fruit in 1-2 years; crops summer and winter
careNotes: Slightly frost-sensitive — protect young trees. Wind protection helpful in exposed locations. Fertilize regularly for best production. 'Arkin' is the most popular Florida variety. Harvest promptly when ripe.

name: Jackfruit
description: The world's largest tree fruit — individual fruits can weigh 10-100 lbs! Extremely productive tropical tree with sweet yellow flesh. Unripe jackfruit has a meaty texture popular as a plant-based meat substitute. Thrives in Zone 10b.
daysToBloom: Grafted trees fruit in 2-3 years; fruits summer
careNotes: Fast-growing — can reach 30-40 ft, give plenty of space. Fruits grow directly from trunk and branches. Wear gloves when harvesting — sticky white latex. 'Black Gold' variety excels in South Florida.

name: Sapodilla
description: Tropical fruit tree producing brown rough-skinned fruits with incredibly sweet honey-like flesh tasting of pear and brown sugar. Beautiful ornamental evergreen. One of the most cold-tolerant tropical fruits in Florida.
daysToBloom: Grafted trees fruit in 3-5 years; year-round production
careNotes: Very salt and drought tolerant. Excellent wind resistance — great for coastal gardens. Harvest when fruit yields slightly to pressure. Fruit ripens off the tree. 'Brown Sugar' and 'Tikal' are top Florida varieties.

name: Sugar Apple
description: Tropical tree producing lumpy green fruits with sweet creamy white flesh tasting of vanilla and custard. A favorite tropical treat widely grown in the Caribbean. Hand-pollination dramatically increases fruit set.
daysToBloom: Grafted trees fruit in 2-3 years; summer fruiting
careNotes: Hand-pollinate afternoon flowers with a small paintbrush for best fruit set. Needs good drainage. Deciduous in mild cold weather. Cold-sensitive — protect from frost. 'African Pride' is a popular Florida variety.

name: Soursop (Guanábana)
description: Large tropical tree producing enormous spiny green fruits with white fibrous flesh that is intensely tropical — a mix of strawberry, pineapple, and citrus. Widely used in smoothies, ice cream, and juices throughout the Caribbean.
daysToBloom: 3-5 years from seed; grafted trees faster; summer fruiting
careNotes: Cold-sensitive — one of the more frost-tender tropical fruits. Benefits from hand-pollination. Harvest when skin changes from dark to light green and flesh gives slightly to pressure. Fast-growing tree.

name: Longan
description: Tropical fruit tree closely related to lychee, producing clusters of round brown fruits with sweet translucent white flesh and a distinctive floral aroma. More heat-tolerant and easier to grow in Zone 10b than lychee.
daysToBloom: Grafted: 2-3 years; blooms spring, fruits July-August
careNotes: 'Kohala' is the top recommended variety for Florida. Benefits from slight drought stress to trigger flowering. Mulch heavily. More forgiving than lychee. Mature trees can produce 200+ lbs per year.

name: Acerola Cherry
description: Tropical shrub/small tree producing small bright-red cherry-like fruits with one of the highest vitamin C concentrations of any fruit — 50-100x more than oranges. Pretty pink flowers and very productive in Zone 10b.
daysToBloom: Fruits within 1-2 years; produces multiple crops per year
careNotes: Fairly cold-tolerant for a tropical fruit. Well-draining soil important. Harvest promptly when red — very perishable. Excellent for juicing, smoothies, and as a natural vitamin C source. Drought-tolerant once established.

name: Pomegranate
description: Deciduous shrub or small tree producing iconic red fruits filled with jewel-like ruby seed arils. Surprisingly well-adapted to Florida — tolerates heat, drought, and mild cold. Beautiful orange-red flowers in spring.
careNotes: 'Wonderful' is the standard variety for Florida. Drought-tolerant once established. Avoid overwatering — causes fruit to split. May lose leaves in winter. Prune to shape after fruiting. Extremely long-lived trees.

name: Fig
description: Ancient deciduous fruit tree producing sweet honey-rich fruits. More adaptable to Florida than most realize. 'Brown Turkey', 'Celeste', and 'LSU Gold' are the best varieties for Zone 10b's heat and humidity.
daysToBloom: Fruits within 1-2 years; main crop August-October
careNotes: Sandy well-draining soil essential. Protect from root-knot nematodes by planting in raised beds. 'Celeste' is the most nematode-resistant variety for Florida. Mulch heavily. Prune while dormant in late winter.

name: Mulberry
description: Fast-growing deciduous tree producing abundant sweet blackberry-like fruits with minimal care. Extremely productive in Florida. Fruits are delicious fresh or made into jams and wines. A magnet for birds.
careNotes: One of the easiest fruit trees for Florida. Very drought-tolerant and fast-growing (can fruit in first year from a cutting). Staining fruits — plant away from driveways and patios. Plant multiple trees for extended harvest.

name: Tamarind
description: Majestic tropical shade tree producing brown pods filled with sweet-sour pulp used in Caribbean, Indian, Thai, and Mexican cuisines. Beautiful feathery foliage and extremely long-lived. One of the best shade trees for Zone 10b.
daysToBloom: 6-8 years from seed; grafted faster; fruits winter through spring
careNotes: Very drought and wind tolerant once established. Fruits best during the dry season. 'Manila Sweet' is a top variety for fresh eating. Extraordinarily long-lived — trees can live hundreds of years.

name: Coconut Palm
description: The iconic tropical palm producing large coconuts filled with sweet water and white flesh. A quintessential Clearwater/Florida landscape element. Extremely salt and wind tolerant. 'Malayan Dwarf' varieties are more disease-resistant.
daysToBloom: 3-8 years to first fruit depending on variety
careNotes: Sandy well-draining soil. Salt-tolerant — thrives at the coast. Fertilize regularly with palm fertilizer (8-4-12 + micronutrients). Watch for lethal bronzing disease. Remove only completely dead fronds — never top a palm.

name: Noni
description: Tropical evergreen tree producing large white fruits with a pungent smell when ripe. Despite the odor, noni juice is consumed worldwide for its medicinal properties. Extremely tough — grows in poor soil, drought, and salt spray.
daysToBloom: 18 months to first fruit; produces year-round thereafter
careNotes: One of the most adaptable trees for Zone 10b — tolerates poor sandy soil, salt spray, drought, and occasional flooding. Harvest when fruit turns white and slightly soft. Very low maintenance.

name: Surinam Cherry
description: Attractive Florida-hardy shrub/small tree producing small ribbed fruits that ripen from green through yellow, orange, and red to nearly black. Tart-sweet flavor with a hint of resin. Excellent for hedges, jams, and juices.
daysToBloom: 2-3 years from seed; fruits spring and fall
careNotes: Extremely easy to grow in Florida — drought, salt, and pest resistant. Can be pruned into a formal hedge. Fully ripe dark red/black fruits are sweetest. Invasive potential in natural areas — harvest fruit promptly.

name: Black Sapote (Chocolate Pudding Fruit)
description: Unique tropical fruit tree producing green-skinned fruits with dark brown flesh that tastes remarkably like chocolate pudding. Related to persimmons. A conversation starter in any garden. Produces heavily in Zone 10b.
daysToBloom: 3-4 years from seed; grafted trees faster; fruits fall-winter
careNotes: Must be fully ripe before eating — unripe fruits are very astringent. Fruit is ripe when skin turns olive/yellow-green and flesh is very soft. Well-draining soil. More cold-tolerant than many tropicals.
